Article.ArticleTitle: High-resolution mapping reveals frequency-specific alterations in phase amplitude coupling after sleep deprivation.

Abstract.AbstractText: Sleep deprivation (SD) is a common problem in modern society and is detrimental to human health, leading to cognitive dysfunctions. Non-invasive neuromodulation techniques have recently shown promise in mitigating the adverse effects of SD. However, their efficacy depends on the stimulation frequency and location. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate the impact of SD on oscillatory activity across specific regions and frequency points, rather than conventional broad frequency bands. In this study, we recorded resting-state electroencephalography after resting wakefulness and SD in 21 healthy young participants. Then, we examined the relative power across the entire spectrum and all channels, and assessed phase-amplitude coupling (PAC) using a sliding-window within continuous low-frequency (1-30 Hz) and high-frequency (2-40 Hz) ranges, instead of fixed, predefined frequency bands. Our results revealed that relative power changes occurred at discrete frequency points in O2 and T3. Bidirectional PAC reconfiguration occurred between multiple distinct low-high frequency pairs after SD-weakened delta/alpha-beta PAC in FP1 and FP2, and enhanced theta/alpha-beta coupling in C4 and O2. Furthermore, the reduced PAC in the frequency pair (1 Hz, 18 Hz) at FP2 positively correlated with the increased 10% fast reaction time of the psychomotor vigilance task after SD, as determined by stepwise regression analysis. In conclusion, these findings suggest that SD results in complex region- and frequency-specific disruptions in brain activity rather than a global broadband changes, and offer promising targets for frequency- and location-specific neuromodulation approaches to alleviate attention deficits induced by sleep loss.
